prompt:
Planner work item for the workflow runner. Documents come to us already validated (the linter upstream guarantees well-formed YAML, correct structure, non-empty do blocks, and jump targets that exist), but the runner still walks raw YAML at execution time, re-resolving names on every step. I want that gone: compile once into a flat plan with numeric jumps. Write flowplan.py — PyYAML is pinned in requirements.txt; set up the project venv with python3 -m venv .venv and .venv/bin/pip install -r requirements.txt (CI runs bash run_verify.sh, which refuses to start without .venv). Exports:

- compile_plan(text: str) -> dict — text is the full workflow document; the planner only cares about the do section.
- class PlanError(Exception) — raised for the two graph problems below, with the exact messages.

The plan is {"tasks": [entry, ...]} in document order. Every entry has: index (its position), name (the task name), type (whichever task-type key the task declares: call, run, set, switch, for, raise, emit, wait), and next — an int index or the string "end" — resolved from the task's then: omitted or "continue" means the following task (or "end" from the last one); "end" means "end"; a task name means that task's index in the SAME block. Every entry gets a next, even types that never use one at runtime (a raise, say) — the plan is a static structure and the runtime picks what it needs. Payload details (call arguments, set values, run specs, expressions in if, ...) are NOT the planner's business; the plan is the control-flow skeleton only.

switch entries additionally carry cases: a list, in document order, of {"when": <the expression string, or None for the default case>, "target": <resolved exactly like then, relative to the switch's own position — so a case then: continue targets the index after the switch>}. The switch's own next is the fall-through (taken at runtime only when no case matches and there is no default) — compute it always, from the switch task's own then.

for entries additionally carry each, in, at (present in every for entry; None when the document omits at), and body: the nested do compiled RECURSIVELY into its own complete plan dict — indices and jump targets inside a body are local to that body, and a then: end inside a body ends the iteration, not the workflow.

Two analyses guard every block (a body is analyzed when it is compiled, so problems surface innermost-first; within one block, cycles are checked before reachability):

1. Jump cycles, over UNCONDITIONAL edges only: each non-switch task has exactly one outgoing edge, its next ("end" means no edge); switch tasks contribute NO unconditional edge — their routing is conditional, which is exactly why a retry loop that jumps back to a switch is legal and must compile. If following unconditional edges ever loops, raise PlanError("jump cycle detected: a -> b -> a"): the task names joined with " -> ", starting and ending at the cycle's lowest-indexed member. If several independent cycles exist, report the one whose lowest-indexed member is smallest.

2. Unreachable tasks, from index 0 over the reachability edges: a non-switch task reaches its next; a switch reaches every case target, plus its fall-through next ONLY when it has no default (with a default, fall-through can never happen). Anything not reached: PlanError("unreachable tasks: b, c") — names in index order, always that exact prefix.

test_flowplan.py is the acceptance contract: bash run_verify.sh must pass, and the tests, run_verify.sh, and requirements.txt stay untouched. Single module.
